data.title: Arena Group: From Turnaround To Growth Story
data.description:
My most successful strategy has been buying companies that have significantly improved without the market yet catching on. I refer to these as turnarounds. The problem is, I only find a handful of these each year. Arena Group Holdings, Inc. (AREN) is a prime example. A year ago, Arena was hemorrhaging money with excessive leverage, and a huge litigation liability. Bankruptcy appeared imminent. Today it’s solidly profitable. The turnaround is complete.

What investors are now missing is this is now a growth story in the new online media space. It appears to have a formula for significant sustainable growth. Yet, the stock trades for only 10x likely earnings. I believe this disconnect is the result of no analyst coverage, a NYSE American listing, and a history, until mid-last year, of large losses.

Background

Arena IPO’d in February 2022 on the NYSE American Exchange. It is an online platform of owned media sites and 150 independent sites that benefit from the advertising dollars and numerous services Arena brings in. The owned sites are Athlon Sports, Men’s Journal, TheStreet, Parade, Surfer, The Spun, and Parade Home & Garden among others. On May 12th, 2025 it added Travelhost for $1 million. The independent publishers incur their own costs to create content and benefit from Arena’s search engine optimization, social media, ad monetization, audience development, and subscription marketing. None of its content is AI written. Almost all revenues are from online publications. Arena still prints some magazines, but more for marketing purposes as it’s not a profit center.

The company used to publish Sports Illustrated under license from Authentic Brands. In early 2024, Arena ceased publication and was sued by Authentic for license payments and other costs. On April 29, 2025, Arena reached a settlement with Authentic Brands, which allowed them to remove all liability to Authentic. On that date, a $93.9 million balance sheet liability as of March 31, 2025, went away. The press release indicated it cost an immaterial amount to settle.

Manoj Bhargava owns 71.4% of the stock through Simplify Inventions, LLC. He served as the interim CEO from December 2023 to January 2024, and co-President February 2024 – April 2024. Mr. Bhargava is the Founder of Innovation Ventures LLC (5-hour ENERGY) and has served as its Chief Executive Officer since its inception. Mr. Bhargava founded 5-hour ENERGY in 2000 and has grown it to annual retail sales of over $1 billion.

Manoj invested $62 million into Arena in the past two years to keep it afloat, obtaining a majority stake. Paul Edmondson was named the new CEO in March 2025. Mr. Edmondson has been with the company since 2018. Four of the 6 directors resigned on April 29, 2025, and 1 new one was brought on. That currently leaves only 3 directors, who do not include Mr. Bhargava. The bylaws call for 3-6 directors. On June 6, 2025, it was announced that Arena had returned to compliance with all of the NYSE American’s continued listing standards. It had fallen out of compliance due to negative net worth and continued losses.

The business is somewhat seasonal with the first quarter being the slowest and the fourth being the busiest. The company currently does not do conference calls. However, in the last 2 quarters, it provided a video answering investor questions.

Financials

Arena lost $56 million in 2023 and $101 million in 2024. The majority of these losses were from now discontinued operations, namely Sports Illustrated. Discontinued operation losses were $121 million in those 2 years, or 77% of the total. That indicates even without Sports Illustrated, Arena was losing a lot of money. Financial results for the last 5 quarters are shown below.

Arena had been losing money for years prior to the third quarter of 2024. Everything changed that quarter due to a change in the business model, though these changes had been in the works for a while. With Sports Illustrated (their largest publication) gone, they were able to right-size the company. The executive ranks were reduced. Legal expenses have been significantly reduced. A large direct sales force was found to be unnecessary. The result was a reduction in staff from around 600 to currently around 160.

Both General & Administrative and Sales & Marketing costs have been cut in half and the gross margin jumped. To give you an idea of the scale of the operating expense cuts, in the most recent quarter, G&A declined year over year from $10.1 million to $5.3 million. S&M declined from $4.6 million to $2.1 million. The higher gross margin is due to changing from a large number of highly paid in-house writers to using more independent writers who are paid based on reader engagement. These reductions appear not only sustainable but there is capacity to grow without large new investment.

Earnings in the last 3 quarters since the major changes were made have totaled $0.33. The guidance for the current quarter indicates earnings of at least $0.12 or more. That puts the EPS current annual run rate at $0.45. However, as shown in the table above, prior earnings were understated due to software amortization exceeding software capex. When factoring that in, the annual EPS run rate is more like $0.50.

Revenue increased 10% last quarter year over year after factoring out Sports Illustrated. Revenues and earnings last quarter sequentially due to seasonality. The first quarter is the slowest and the fourth quarter the busiest. The guidance discussed below indicates growth is actually expected to significantly accelerate.

Balance Sheet

The balance sheet has improved significantly over the past year but remains weak. Tangible net worth improved from -$224 million on June 30, 2024, to -$97 million as of March 31, 2025, after eliminating the litigation liability. Most of the rest of the improvement was capital raises from Manoj Bhargava and profits in the last 3 quarters. Currently, management’s primary use of cash flow is to pay down debt with a secondary use being small acquisitions. The $111 million of debt shown above comes due in December 2026. However, it is owed to a company controlled by Manoj Bhargava, a related party. Strong recent profits should lead to a refinancing at a significantly lower interest rate.

Guidance

Arena had not provided financial guidance in the past but did so for the first time last quarter. They guided for $42.5 million in revenues this quarter (2Q 2025), at the midpoint, and operating income of $10 million. The $10 million operating income is well above the $7.4 million in the first quarter of 2025. The revenue guidance is 56% above that realized in 2Q 2024. In the stockholder video about the quarter, Manoj stated he expects a $12 stock price by year-end.

Catalysts

This is no longer just a turnaround story, it’s increasingly a growth story too with a 56% revenue increase guide for this quarter. The catalysts to improve revenues, profits, and the stock price are listed below.

1. New Business Model - The primary catalyst is a change in the business model. The traditional media model was to find the best writers you could afford in order to put out the best product. Arena’s new model is actually more like Seeking Alpha. Writers are paid based on the number of readers they get. Arena goes beyond this by helping their independent writers businesses in other ways discussed earlier. The Sports Illustrated publication failed for Arena because readers were no longer willing to pay for the best writers. This new model was first applied to Arena’s now primary sports site, Athlon Sports.

Athlon’s audience traffic grew over 500% in Q1 2025 versus Q1 2024. Syndication and commerce revenue for Athlon grew 730% year over year versus Q1 2024. Website traffic to Athlon as measured by semrush.com is shown below.

Arena has now committed to bring its other owned sites to this new model.

Men’s Journal was recently converted to the new model. Its traffic increased 282% over the previous month to 33.1 million page views in March 2025, the first month the new business model was applied.

TheStreet also had record traffic levels in March 2025 with 80 million page views, up 100% versus March 2024.

2. Lower Interest Rates - There is a major opportunity to significantly reduce interest expense. Arena is currently paying about 11% on its debt, which is mostly to Manoj Bhargava’s company. That debt matures in December 2026. However, the debt was incurred when Arena wasn’t profitable. With 3 quarters of profits now, they should be able to refinance to a much lower interest rate. They may be able to reduce interest expense by at least $4 million per year. That would be a $0.02 improvement in EPS per quarter.

3. Bolt-on Acquisitions – The price of many legacy magazine publishers is dropping and many names with brand equity can now be acquired for little money. In May 2025, Arena acquired the venerable Travelhost brand for $1 million. They expect to apply their performance business model to it. Arena’s return to solid profitability will allow it to pursue more small bolt-on deals. Management has said they would like to do 4 of these smaller bolt-ons a year. These acquisitions are likely to be lower risk as they are mostly buying just the names with few other assets, liabilities or costs.

4. Performance Marketing – This revenue line item found in Note 17 (Segment Reporting) of the footnotes jumped to $4.7 million in the most recent quarter from $0.3 million a year earlier. This is marketing content prepared for commercial customers. Arena is paid based on consumer purchases done through that marketing content.

5. Syndicating Content – Arena has recently gotten significant new revenues syndicating content to third-party sites. This revenue has very high margins.

6. Low Capex – Arena can grow significantly without a lot of investment. It has relatively few fixed asset needs and little inventory to fund. Its main investment is in software. The platform they need to grow existing media properties and acquire new ones is already in place. Having the capacity to grow without significant investment usually leads to earnings growth exceeding revenue growth.

7. Up-listing – Arena would benefit from an up-listing to the NYSE or Nasdaq. This would add liquidity and exposure to its stock. It is currently listed on the NYSE American Exchange.

8. Tax Loss Carryforwards – Arena had $210 million of tax loss carryforwards as of December 31, 2024. That should be sufficient to shelter profits for many years to come.

9. AI Training Fees – Though not mentioned by Arena, this is a possible revenue source in the future. The New York Times, among others, have already done this. Curiosity Stream, a smaller company I used as a comparable in the Valuation section below, is doing this in a big way.

Concerns

Arena still has a large negative tangible net worth. After adjusting for the settlement with Authentic, it was -$96.7 million as of March 31, 2025.

Arena is primarily reliant on advertising for revenue. That could be impacted in a recession.

Four directors resigned in April 2025. These directors had been brought on board when Arena was struggling and left once stabilized.

Technicals

As shown in the chart below, AREN stock jumped from $0.57 to $1.75 on November 15th, 2024, when the first quarterly profit was reported for the third quarter of 2024. It had been trading at bankruptcy levels. It then jumped again to as high as $9.76 on May 19, 2025, after three major positive announcements. These were two more strong quarters and the announcement of the settlement of litigation with Authentic Brands. But then the stock backtracked by half to the $5.17 it is today. I believe some of this backtracking is technical. That is the traders moving on from what had been a hot stock.

Some may say that an 800% increase since last November means the easy money has been made. But it’s not about where it’s been but what the upside is from here. The stock move so far reflects going from imminent bankruptcy to profitable but average. But the prospects here are a lot better than that.

Valuation

My usual method of valuation is to compare the subject company to its peers and then make adjustments for things like growth, leverage, and profit margins. I wasn’t able to find enough good comparables, so I added a second valuation method.

Competitors listed in the last 10-K are; Vice, BuzzFeed, Business Insider, WordPress, Medium, RebelMouse, Arc, Leaf Group, Future PLC, Fortune, CNN, ESPN, Yahoo!, Google, YouTube, Twitter, Reddit, and Liberty Alliance. The last 8 are way larger and include other businesses and are not comparable for valuation purposes. Of the others, only BuzzFeed and Future PLC are publicly traded. BuzzFeed is not currently profitable, making it difficult to use for valuation purposes. Newsmax recently IPO’d, but there is insufficient history at this point.

Probably the best comparable is Curiosity Stream (CURI) which has a very similar recent history to Arena. Both were left for dead a year ago and changed their business model and cut costs to become profitable. Both stocks have strongly rallied and fallen back some. CURI is a video media company and is smaller. It trades for 5.0x estimated 2025 revenues. Future PLC trades for 1x estimated 2025 revenues. These are very far apart, but I believe this indicates the upside potential for CURI is much higher than Future PLC. Future PLC has had stagnant revenues. I believe Arena should be valued between the two. Its revenue growth prospects are actually above CURI, but that is offset for now by a weak balance sheet. Keep in mind they are guiding for a 56% revenue growth this quarter. Based on the two comparables, I put Arena’s value at 3.0x revenues, which look likely to exceed $150 million this year. That puts the market cap at $450 million which rounds to $9.50 per share.

For a second methodology, I compared Arena to its industry sector. The consumer discretionary sector (XLY) has an average PE ratio of 32 right now according to the Wall Street Journal. The stocks in the XLY are all much larger and mostly better capitalized than Arena. Arena does have similar if not better growth prospects. Discounting for size and capitalization, I get a PE of 20. That would indicate a stock price of $10. That is also below the average U.S. PE Ratio right now of 25, despite having at least as good of growth prospects as the average.

Splitting the two methods, my 1-year price target for Arena Group is $9.75, which is a bit below the $12 year-end guidance provided by Manoj Bhargava.

Takeaway

Arena was a stock priced for bankruptcy 7 months ago. It has since changed its business model, eliminated a huge litigation liability from its balance sheet, and reported 3 strongly profitable quarters. More catalysts are in process as the company moves its new business model to all of its owned brands, acquires cheap unwanted legacy magazines, and has the opportunity to significantly lower interest expenses. If the performance publishing model continues to work as well as it has in its first few months, my price target may need to be doubled.

I believe Arena is still undiscovered, due to no analyst coverage, a poor track record until last year, and not trading on the NYSE or Nasdaq. A recent rally has been cut in half as traders moved on after it stalled. This provides a window of opportunity for investors to get into a highly profitable, solid-growth online media company majority owned by a proven entrepreneur.

data.title: Conflict In Iran: What This Means For The U.S. Economy And Markets
data.description:
There are a number of signs that indicate the U.S. economy is slowing down, and yet there are also some factors that could be poised to keep inflation and interest rates at relatively high levels. These dynamics could lead to "stagflation" which is a period in which 3 factors are typically present and those are: 1) economic growth is stagnant, 2) inflation is higher than desired, and also 3) unemployment is rising. We have had inflation that's been too high, but it has come down in recent months. However, there are concerns that inflation will be reignited by President Trump's tariffs, and with a newly launched attack in the Middle East, oil prices are surging, which could also add to inflationary pressures. Higher oil prices could also be another factor that could slow down the economy and lead it to stagnation. The only missing factor might be higher unemployment rates, but there are signs this could be on the way. Let's take a closer look at these factors as well as what has historically done well in terms of investments during periods of stagflation in the past:

Surging Oil Prices, High Tariffs And Debt Levels Could Reignite Stagflation

In the 1970s, oil price shocks were a major factor in triggering that era of stagflation which was challenging for investors. With the hopes for a nuclear/peace deal with Iran fading fast and the chances of escalation in the Middle East very possible, the price of oil could go much higher in the coming weeks. Oil prices have surged by about 6% in the aftermath of the recent military strikes on Iran, and that is without any oil facilities seemingly being damaged or interrupted. With Israel suggesting there is likely more to come in order to disable Iran's nuclear program, this could provide a risk premium to the oil markets or even cause a major spike if supplies are interrupted. Oil prices have an impact on just about everything in the global economy since many products are made using energy and are shipped as well. A sustained spike in oil acts like a tax, and this will act as an inflationary pressure and also likely reduce global growth.

While the market has recovered much of the losses it sustained after Liberation Day, the potential damage from higher tariffs has yet to be felt completely. Many companies and consumers stocked up on inventories and goods, which has probably created a pull-forward of demand for many items. That means demand could fall sharply since the demand spike is probably unsustainable, and especially so when tariff-related price increases eventually hit the market. If demand for major items like cars and appliances drops sharply in the second half of this year, it will be a drag on growth, and it could help fuel a stagflationary environment.

The rising national debt could keep interest rates high and that could put pressure on the economy, creating a stagnant growth environment for many companies. The world is awash in debt and the bond vigilantes are demanding more for their money when investing in bonds, especially when it comes to long-term debt. Higher interest rates will keep pressure on the housing market and the economy in general, all of which could also contribute to a period of stagflation.

The Labor Market Could Be Cracking

In May 2025, the national unemployment rate was at about 4.2%, which is a relatively strong headline number. However, unemployment claims in June 2025, have risen to the highest level in 8 months. This could be one of the first indicators that the labor market is cooling, and when these trends start, they tend to go much further than most people and economists expect. Recent job creation numbers have been showing signs of weakness as well. The combination of rising unemployment claims, reduced job creation, and uncertainty caused by tariffs could make consumers and companies more cautious in the coming months. Historically, a weakening job market is an early warning sign that often occurs before an economic slowdown.

It's also worth noting that there have been many government job cuts because of DOGE, but it seems like the cost-cutting and efficiency theme has inspired many very healthy and profitable companies to announce job cuts. In recent weeks we have seen layoffs in healthy companies such as Autodesk (ADSK), and Alphabet (GOOGL), (GOOG), and I expect there are more to come, especially in companies that are impacted by tariffs.

Another factor that could be starting to impact the job market is AI and humanoid robotics. These new developments could lead to significant job losses in the coming years. I believe AI is already starting to replace some jobs, which could be impacting the jobs market now. Humanoid robotics could replace many jobs in the not-so-distant future. This could impact wage growth and consumer spending.

The Market Has Rebounded Too Far And Too Fast

As the chart of the S&P 500 Index (NYSEARCA:SPY) below shows, the market has rebounded and is close to record highs. I believe this rebound has gone too far and too fast, considering that the complete impact of higher tariffs has not likely been felt yet. I also think the optimism over trade deals could be premature. In addition, the risk of stagflation appears to be growing and there are multiple geopolitical risks that could take the stock market lower.

What Outperforms And Underperforms During Stagflationary Times

Oil, gold, and hard assets tend to outperform during periods of stagflation. In terms of paper assets, owning shares of utilities, consumer staples, and value stocks could make sense since these typically do well as investors seek defensive sectors. Of course, cash and short-term bonds could be another place to hide.

Growth stocks and longer-term bonds tend to do poorly during stagflation. If I owned high-flying momentum stocks, I would consider paring back or cashing in now.

Where I Could Be Wrong

AI appears to be a powerful force and possibly unlike anything we have seen in our lifetimes. As such, we might be underestimating how much this new dynamic could boost economic growth. Also, many Middle East conflicts have been resolved within a relatively short time in the past, and that has led to only short-term price spikes in oil, in most cases. If the recent spike in oil is not sustained, this might take us one step back from potential stagflation. However, that may not be enough, and I think the situation facing the regime in Iran is existential. This leads me to think that the conflict will escalate, especially since Iran has decided to end nuclear deal talks. If the Iranian people rise up to topple the regime in Iran, this could be very positive for stocks and send oil prices back down.

In Summary

I believe this new conflict is far more serious than what we have seen historically. This is because facing an existential crisis, the Iranian regime might escalate matters, and that could lead to higher for longer in terms of oil prices. In addition, there are multiple signs the U.S. economy is slowing down and there are also cracks in the labor market. The thing about the labor market is that it can seem strong, and then suddenly it can give way and catch economists and investors off guard. It seems that all the factors needed to cause a period of stagflation are either here now, or likely on the horizon. With the stock market at elevated levels, I believe it makes sense to be more cautious and consider assets that outperform during periods of stagflation.

No guarantees or representations are made. Hawkinvest is not a registered investment advisor and does not provide specific investment advice. The information is for informational purposes only. You should always consult a financial advisor.

data.title: Coles: A Steady Grocery Player In Post-Hike Australia, Though Fully Priced
data.description:
In the highly concentrated grocery retail market "Down Under", the Hawthorn East, Australia-based chain Coles Group (OTCPK:CLEGF) holds the second-largest market share in the country (28%), just behind Woolworths (OTCPK:WOLWF) with 37%, which practically controls 65% of the entire market in Australia.

The characteristic of Coles' business, as it is part of the consumer staples segment, is, in theory, non-cyclical, with inelastic demand and generally resilient in periods of economic downturn. For this reason, much of the investment thesis is based on resilience and shareholder returns, and Coles has distributed attractive dividends.

Although over the last two years, with inflation peaking in the post-pandemic scenario and Australia's central bank raising rates to historic levels, Coles' business has not been so immune, and its share price performance has fluctuated in line with the broader market. Likewise, the company's margins and comparable sales followed similar patterns.

But in any case, Coles continues to generate great value for its shareholders, above its cost of capital, showing robust fundamentals within a sector characterized by high-volume and tight margins.

Of course, this comes at a cost. Coles' shares are not particularly cheap, and trade above its main peers. Although the premium is not unfounded, I believe that valuations may be indicating at least a well-priced business, but one that is worth monitoring for any corrections, and from the perspective of including it in a dividend portfolio.

(Note: Coles trades on the OTC markets as an unsponsored ADR. Therefore, its liquidity and information are limited. For more information, I recommend following the ticker on the Australian Stock Exchange (ASX) by the ticker “COL”.)

A Quick Overview Of Coles And The Australian Retail Grocery Market Landscape

If you're in Australia and need to go grocery shopping, you have the following options: Coles—being the chain with the widest diversification of products and brands, and competitive, moderate prices; Woolworths (or Woolies)—the largest chain by market share, the widest variety of products (and potentially the best in-store experience) but with prices that are often more expensive than Coles; then Aldi—the German chain which sells mostly own brands at a more affordable price; Metcash (IGA) (OTCPK:MCSHF), more focused on smaller-format stores but with prices generally higher than those of large chains; and Costco (COST) which has around 15 warehouse locations in major cities.

Above all, this shows that the supermarket sector in Australia is highly concentrated and has high barriers to entry.

Specifically on the Coles side, supermarkets are obviously its core business, but it also operates on the liquor front, under the Liquorland brand, and also operates convenience stores in petrol stations (Express), although it has divested its fuel business.

The performance of Coles' shares on the ASX (Australian stock exchange), in line with Australian equities, represented by the iShares MSCI Australia ETF (EWA) has engaged in a strong rally since the beginning of last year, driven largely by reasons that I attribute to the prospects of a reduced inflation scenario (currently at 2.4% and within the central bank's target) and falling cash rates (at 3.85% from 4.1% early this year).

Coles: competitive positioning metrics

More specifically, with regard to the Coles business, I believe that it is first crucial to analyze its operational quality and competitive resilience. And the first step is to track Coles' top line and bottom-line performance over the last five years.

As the graph above shows, Coles' Group (Supermarket+Liquor) increased steadily from ~AUD 37B in 2020 to over AUD 44B in 2024, despite a slight dip in 2022 as the pandemic boom eased with the economic reopening, but managed to rebound strongly in 2023 and 2024.

At the same time, Coles Group's operating margins peaked in 2021 during the pandemic at ~4.85%, had a natural decline in 2022, but only in 2023, at the height of monetary tightening, bottomed out at ~4.55% until recovering again in 2024 to ~4.7%. These swings may seem irrelevant at first glance, but in a sector that usually has razor-thin margins, an increase of 0.15 or 0.2 percentage points represents millions of AUD in additional profits, given the huge scale of revenues.

It also shows that, despite Coles' theoretically being an anti-cyclical company in the face of macro headwinds, the swing in operating margins coincided with the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) starting an aggressive cash rate hike cycle, where cash rates went from 0.1% before the pandemic to as high as 4.35% in 2023. And this has resulted in strong pressure from grocery chains like Coles not to pass on 100% of inflation to the consumer, which squeezes margins.

So, as we can see, the recovery in margins in 2024 clearly showed a respite for Coles as inflation began to slow and interest rate rises stabilized. The 2024 recovery indicates better cost control, productivity improvements, or improved sales mix.

The gains in margins and efficiency last year are also attributable to the investments Coles has continued to make over the last five years. The company invested around AU$6 billion in CapEx in order to modernize its supply chain, distribution, and fulfillment centers. As a result, Coles has managed to achieve fulfillment rates of 99% and has also managed to increase its product range by a quarter compared to a typical Coles store.

The other metric I'd like to focus on is Coles' same-store sales growth. As the graph below shows, supermarket comps sales fell from ~6% in 2020 during the pandemic to as low as 2.5% between 2021-2022, but returned to 5.5% in 2023 mainly with product availability and customer retention initiatives (also attributable to store investments), despite a brief correction in 2024, but still at rates below the pandemic period.

The most negative side has been the comp sales in liquor. There was a decline from ~7% in 2020 to nearly -1.5% in 2024. This performance, I think, is more serious because it is a reflection of not only increased competition, but also changing consumer behavior and regulatory taxes harming the liquor industry in Australia. Therefore, I attribute structural headwinds to the underperformance from 2022 onwards.

Now, bringing it back to more recent results, in 3Q25, reported in April this year, Coles saw supermarket comp sales growing at 3%, down from 4.7% in the same period last year, but with an acceleration in e-commerce sales jumping 25.7% from 3Q24, already making up more than 11% of the company's total revenues.

On the liquidity side, a brief recovery in comp sales, falling just 2% versus 3.1% in the same period last year, can also be attributed to greater penetration of digital channels.

According to the management team, expectations for the 4Q25 results are as follows:

“In the early part of the fourth quarter, Supermarkets sales growth has remained broadly in line with the third quarter with continued volume growth supported by a positive response from customers across our Easter offer. In Liquor, we have continued to see positive sales growth underpinned by the success of our recent value campaigns.”

A Brief Look At Coles’ Financial Health

For a capital-intensive, low-margin business like Coles, solvency, capital efficiency, and cash generation are key to having a broader vision of how the business has returned value to its shareholders.

In this case, Coles has been able to efficiently transform its invested capital into profits, as it has had an expressive Return on Capital (ROC)—calculated from EBIT/Capital Employed (total assets minus current liabilities). Over the last five years, Coles has had an average ROC of 16.3%, increasing by 1% from 2021 to 2025, to recently reach 16.6%. Considering that Coles has an estimated cost of equity of ~6%, given a low beta and risk-free rates of 3.85%, the ROC is well above its cost of capital, indicating consistent value creation for its shareholders.

The second point is Coles' liquidity and leverage. Currently, the supermarket chain has a conservative capital structure with a balance sheet leverage ratio of 2.7x, less leveraged than five years ago when it was 3.1x, but at the same time slightly below the 2.6x in 2023.

The final, yet equally important, metric I bring up is cash flows. Coles' cash realization ratio has exceeded 100% in the last five years, with the exception of last year, in 2024, when it stood at 98%. In this case, that implies a drop following the extent to which Coles had been able to convert its earnings into actual cash. In this case, the drop was attributable to rising working capital, the result of inventory build-up.

But in general, the AU$2.8 billion/year in operating cash reiterates the slack in remunerating shareholders without having to take on debt or sell assets, which makes Coles a good dividend payer. Over the last five years, Coles' payout has ranged from 81% of its profits, increasing dividends over the last six years, and currently paying a yield of 3.17%—just below the risk-free rate in Australia.

Risks And Final Considerations

The retail grocery landscape in Australia is highly competitive, and price wars tend to be the primary risk of the thesis, leading to potential market share erosion and margin pressures. Furthermore, the ongoing trade wars tend to spill over into the sector as a whole, and Coles, being no exception, might not be able to pass all cost increases onto customers while keeping operating profits intact.

However, taking into account that in the last twelve months, Coles' operating profits totaled AU$1.98 billion while the company trades at an enterprise value of AU$39.5 billion, this equated to an earnings yield (EBIT/EV) of 5%, being, in theory, lower than its cost of capital and suggesting a stock at least fairly priced.

Nor does Coles' ADR trade below its main domestic peers Woolworths and Metcash, although it pays a dividend yield about the same as its main competitor—Woolworths—and boasts traditional operating metrics of lower ROIC/ROCE at 9%/14.4% vs. 13%/21%, respectively.

That said, for valuation reasons, I see Coles' ADR as a Hold at present, even though the company should continue to return value to its shareholders above its cost of capital.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: An Innovative Approach To Benchmarking Canadian Credit Risk
data.description:
By Joshua Gorelik, Senior Manager, Quantitative Product Research, Fixed Income, Currencies and Commodities | Marina Mets, Head of Fixed Income, Currencies and Commodities, Americas

Introduction

FTSE Russell is introducing an innovative index solution that is designed to capture the dynamics of Canadian corporate credit risk. Through extensive research and in-depth analysis, we have constructed a liquid, simple and transparent benchmark that isolates the credit spread component in Canadian bank bonds to provide a standardised gauge of credit market conditions in Canada.

The FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series captures the yield differential (spread) between a basket of domestic Canadian bank bonds and benchmark Government of Canada securities, serving as a measure of Canadian financial sector credit risk.

The indices within the series are transparent and designed to be replicable, with individual security holdings, prices and spreads disclosed daily. In this way, the index series addresses the need for representative spread products focused on the Canadian fixed income market.

In this paper, we show how the design of the index series balances capacity, tradeability and the accuracy of spread measurement. We explain the indices’ construction methodology and look at simulated past performance, showing that the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index can act as a hedge for a broad range of corporate bond exposures.

Functions of bank credit spreads

Corporate bonds’ credit spreads are often used as leading indicators for economic activity because of the relationship between the quality of borrowers' balance sheets and their access to external finance[1].

For example, credit spreads started to rise sharply from summer 2007 onwards[2], over a year before the default of Lehman Brothers, serving as an advance warning of deteriorating financial market conditions.

Within the corporate bond sector, banks’ credit spreads merit particular attention. Given the high repeat issuance levels and relatively higher liquidity of bank-issued corporate bonds, market shocks or periods of credit spread compression are usually captured more quickly in banks than in the broader corporate bond universe.

This is especially true of Canada, where banks represent a large proportion of the overall corporate sector of the FTSE Canada Universe Bond Index[3], a flagship measure of the Canadian bond market.

An index that quickly reflects market perceptions of bank credit risk therefore serves as a valuable tool not just for corporate bond investors, but for the broader financial markets. Such an index can serve as a benchmark for broader credit market conditions, a key risk measure in asset allocation models and as a pricing tool in other financial contracts (such as listed derivatives).

Exhibit 1: Functions of bank credit spreads

Design principles for a bank credit spread index

FTSE Russell is committed to fostering evolution in the markets we serve. We have been working closely with key industry partners to support the development of credit risk transfer solutions for the Canadian market. Through extensive research and in-depth analysis, we have designed a liquid, simple and transparent benchmark that isolates the credit spread component in bank-issued corporate debt to provide a standardised gauge of credit market conditions in Canada.

To ensure broad adoption and ease of use across a variety of market applications, FTSE’s approach to constructing the FTSE Canada Credit Spread Index series was guided by several considerations:

Liquidity: the basket of index securities should be highly liquid;

Simplicity: the index’s construction methodology and weight allocation should be easy to understand; and

Transparency: the index’s selection methodology should be replicable by index users and the index data should be available for access.

Building the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series

The construction of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series involves three stages. These are designed to address the need for a domestic representative spread product that isolates credit risk exposure while focusing on liquid securities, balancing capacity, tradeability and spread movement.

First, we create a reference basket of bank bonds, drawing the most liquid components from the FTSE Canada Corporate Financial Bank Bond index, part of the FTSE Canada Universe Bond index.

Second, we map the bonds in the reference basket to benchmark Government of Canada bonds.

Finally, we calculate the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index as the spread between the reference basket and the basket of parent bonds.

1 - Creation of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket

The selection process for the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket is highlighted in the infographic. We set the base universe, narrow it using a series of requirements designed to exclude less liquid or non-homogeneous issues, bucket the remaining securities by term and seniority and then weight them by market capitalisation.

Exhibit 2: Creation of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket

The reference basket is set at the end of February, May, August, and November and remains constant over each following quarter. If a security in the reference basket is subject to a default or a call by the issuer, it is removed on the last business day prior to the effective corporate action and its weight in the basket is reinvested proportionally into the remaining basket securities. If a security is downgraded to below investment grade, it remains in the basket until the next quarterly rebalancing date.

2 - Mapping to Government of Canada securities

At the quarterly index rebalancing date, each corporate bond in the reference basket (a “child”) is mapped to a benchmark Government of Canada bond (a “parent”). This mapping is based on the parent-child mapping scheme sourced from CanDeal Data and Analytics. The weights of the Government of Canada bonds are set as equal to the mapped corporate bonds, and the mapping is reported in the index files for transparency. A mapping validation is performed before each index rebalancing date to ensure that all bank bonds have a valid Government of Canada benchmark parent at that date.

Exhibit 3: Examples of parent-child mapping for FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index

3 - Calculation and aggregation of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index

Once the parent-child mapping is complete for the bonds in the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket, we calculate the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series as follows:

  • Spread value is calculated daily at 4 pm (using mid-market closing price) for each position in the basket;
  • Spread is calculated as yield-to-worst (YTW) on bank-issued corporate bond – YTW on Government of Canada bond;
  • Spread index is the market capitalisation-weighted average spread, based on the weights of the reference basket;
  • Spread index value serves as the basis for the listed futures contract on the index (see the section below, “Spread indices as the basis for futures contracts”).

Summary statistics for FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket

A back-test between June 2012 and March 2025 of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket methodology shows that the basket closely tracks the broader Canadian financials sector as measured by yield, spread, and duration. In recent history, the average coupon of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket has reflected the higher prevailing rates for newer bank issuances, given that the reference basket selects the most recently issued bond per issuer within each sector group (the sector groups are categorised by remaining term and seniority).

Exhibit 4: Summary statistics for FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket

Correlations of spread index to financials and corporates

A back-test of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series methodology over the period from June 2012 to March 2025 also showed high correlations with the Canadian financials sector and with the corporate bond sector overall. This is unsurprising, given that Canadian bank bonds typically represent approximately 70% by weight of the financial sector (while financials, in turn, typically represent approximately 45% of the corporate bond market).

In other words, the implied spread embedded in the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index serves as a strong proxy for Canada’s corporate bond and financial bond sectors, with comparable levels of volatility.

Exhibit 5: Volatility and correlations of FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index

Please note:

  1. Spreads for Canada Corporate, Financial, Reference Basket, and US Bank are calculated using month-end index-level OAS measures.
  2. Spreads for Canada Bank Credit Spread Index are calculated as the weighted average YTW difference between bonds in the Reference Basket and their corresponding Government of Canada bonds.
  3. Spread volatility is calculated as the standard deviation of spread change.
  4. Implied spreads for Canada Corporate and Financial are calculated as the YTW difference measured against the Short-Term Federal Non-Agency Bond Index.
  5. For US Banks, daily volatility is inferred from monthly volatility, assuming 21 trading days per month

Spread indices as the basis for futures contracts

A new FTSE Canada Credit Spread index series will be created quarterly on the last business day of February, May, August, and November, based on the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket. Each spread index series will expire four months after creation, in alignment with the life of the futures contract written on the series.

The futures contracts are expected to originate and settle between International Monetary Market (IMM) dates, which are the third Wednesday of each March, June, September, and December[4].

The FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series naming convention will include a three-character code indicating the delivery date. This will follow the standard ticker convention observed in the futures market, with the reference letters H, M, U, and Z corresponding to March, June, September, and December, respectively. For example:

  • FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index H25 (ticker: CBCH25) for an index series created on the last business day of November 2024 and maturing at the end of March 2025;
  • FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index M25 (ticker: CBCM25) for an index series created on the last business day of February 2025 and maturing at the end of June 2025;
  • FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index U25 (ticker: CBCU25) for an index series created on the last business day of May 2025 and maturing at the end of September 2025;
  • FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Z25 (ticker: CBCZ25) for an index series created on the last business day of August 2025 and maturing at the end of December 2025.

Exhibit 6: Sample timeline for reference basket rebalancing, index series, and related futures contracts

A risk transfer solution for the Canadian market

FTSE Russell is proud that the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series has been selected by the Montreal Exchange to serve as the foundation for a first-of-its-kind credit derivatives product, the Canada Bank Credit Index Futures (CBC), which will launch in 2026. These futures will provide market participants with a powerful tool to manage credit exposure, hedge risk and implement strategic investment views.

The futures are expected to serve a variety of uses:

  • Targeted credit exposure (as a way to gain direct exposure to the credit spread of Canadian banks)
  • Expanded yield curve access (by adding new, liquid points to the Canadian-listed yield curve, enhancing curve construction and trading precision)
  • Efficient hedging tool (to help manage spread risk, portfolio duration and liquidity needs)
  • Versatile trading strategies (to implement tactical credit views, replicate synthetic long/short credit positions or execute relative value trades)

Addressing Canadian client needs

FTSE Russell has created the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index to address the needs of Canadian financial market participants for a representative spread product that isolates domestic banks’ credit risk exposure while focusing on their most liquid fixed income securities. The index’s design balances capacity, tradeability and offer insights into credit risk dynamics within the Canadian investment grade market, facilitating informed decision-making for investors.

Footnotes

1 Credit spreads, economic activity and fragmentation.

2 See Timeline: The credit crunch of 2007/2008 | Reuters.

3 On average, we observed that financial bonds represent 44.7% of the all corporate sector of the FTSE Canada Universe Bond Index, where bank bonds represent 29.4% and 65.9% of the all corporate and financial sectors, respectively, based on daily data for the period 31 December 2003 through 31 May 2025. Source: FTSE Russell.

4 See FTSE Canada Bank Credit Futures (CBC) for more details.

Manager Commentary

“Major indexes experienced notable declines due to escalating trade tensions (including retaliatory measures from key trading partners), continued tariff policy uncertainty, and increasingly cautionary investor sentiment.” - Harbor Capital Advisors, Inc.

Market in Review

The U.S. equity market faced significant challenges in the first quarter of 2025. Major indexes experienced notable declines due to escalating trade tensions (including retaliatory measures from key trading partners), continued tariff policy uncertainty, and increasingly cautionary investor sentiment.

Portfolio Performance

During the first quarter, the Harbor Human Capital Factor US Large Cap ETF (“Fund”) returned -4.13%, (NAV) slightly underperforming its benchmark, the CIBC Human Capital Index, which returned -4.02%. The S&P 500 Index returned -4.27% during the period.

The Fund employs an indexing investment approach designed to track the performance of the CIBC Human Capital Index.

Contributors and Detractors

The top five relative detractors from performance include lack of exposure to Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (Class B), Alphabet (GOOGL) (Class A), Marvell Technology (MRVL), Constellation Energy (CEG), and a 0% weighting in Exxon Mobil (XOM).

The largest contributors to relative performance against the S&P 500 Index include lack of exposure to Tesla (TSLA) and Broadcom (AVGO). Exposure to Amazon (AMZN), NVIDIA (NVDA), and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) was also contributive.

Buys and Sells

In February 2025, the Fund went through its annual reconstitution process in conjunction with CIBC Human Capital Index’s rescreening of companies that possess strong human capital scores, based on Irrational Capital’s proprietary research. At that time, 49 names were removed from the index and 48 were added. The largest names added included Disney (DIS), Visa (V), and Home Depot (HD), while Amazon, Alphabet (Class C), Amgen (AMGN), BlackRock (BLK) and Blackstone (BX) were removed.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Past performance is net of management fees and expenses and reflects reinvested dividends and distributions. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and when redeemed may be worth more or less than their original cost. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Current performance may be higher or lower and is available through the most recent month end at harborcapital.com or by calling 800-422-1050.

Shares are bought and sold at market price not net asset value (NAV). A fund’s NAV is the sum of all its assets less any liabilities, divided by the number of shares outstanding. Market price returns are based upon the closing composite market price and do not represent the returns you would receive if you traded shares at other times.

IMPORTANT INFORMATION

Risks

Investing involves risk, principal loss is possible. Unlike mutual funds, ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to their net asset value. Harbor ETFs are new and have limited operating history to judge.

There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. Stock markets are volatile and equity values can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market and economic conditions. The Fund may not exactly track the performance of the Index with perfect accuracy at all times. Tracking error may occur because of pricing differences, timing and costs incurred by the fund or during times of heightened market volatility.

The Fund relies on the Index provider's methodology in assessing whether a company may be considered a corporate culture leader. There is no guarantee that the construction methodology will accurately assess a company to include or exclude it from the index which could have an adverse effect on the Fund's returns. The Fund's assets may be concentrated in a particular sector or industries to the extent the Index is concentrated and is subject to the risk that economic, political, or other market conditions that have a negative effect on that sector or industry will negatively impact the value of the Fund.

Companies in the information technology sector can be significantly affected by short product cycles, obsolescence of existing technology, impairment or loss of intellectual property rights, falling prices and profits, competition from new market entrants, government regulation and other factors.

Benchmarks

The CIBC Human Capital Index consists of a modified market-weighted portfolio of the equity securities of U.S. companies identified by Irrational Capital LLC (“Irrational Capital”) as those it believes to possess strong corporate culture based on its proprietary scoring methodology. Constituents eligible are chosen from Solactive GBS United States 500 Index (the “index universe”) at the time of Index reconstitution. The Solactive GBS United States 500 Index intends to track the performance of the largest 500 companies from the US stock market. The index listed is unmanaged and does not reflect fees and expenses and is not available for direct investment.

The S&P 500 Index is an unmanaged index generally representative of the U.S. market for large capitalization equities. This unmanaged index does not reflect fees and expenses and is not available for direct investment.

Disclosures

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expense.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments.

Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit harborcapital.com or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

Attribution Disclosures

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3 Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares.

The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS. Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation.

Return on Equity (ROE) is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.
