data.title: Carlyle Secured Lending: Valuation Collapse Makes This BDC A Buy
data.description:
In this article, we catch up on the Q1 results of Carlyle Secured Lending (CGBD). CGBD trades at a dividend yield of 11.5% and a 16% discount to NAV. Its net investment income price yield is 11.8% - in line with the median level. We last covered the stock here.

CGBD has a fairly typical BDC portfolio profile with an 89% first lien allocation. Its median company EBITDA of $87m is also in the sweet spot of the middle-market segment. Its sector exposure is tilted to Software and Healthcare - fairly common in the BDC space.

Quarter Update

Net investment income fell to $0.41. The company declared a dividend of $0.40 for the second quarter, keeping the same base dividend but zeroing out the previous supplemental dividend. The NII drop from last quarter is overstated due to non-recurring income of $0.02 in Q4, meaning the core Q1 drop is not as steep as it looks.

The repayment of the company's lower cost bond in Q4 also weighed on income, as did the merger given the nearly 100% first-lien allocation of CSL III. There is $0.85 of spillover income or more than 2x the base dividend.

CGBD has a supplemental dividend framework - in line with what several other BDCs have done, such as OBDC and GBDC. Specifically, the company will pay out at least half of the excess net income (i.e. adjusted net income less base dividend) through the supplemental dividend. At this point, there is not much excess income for the supplemental to be paid out.

The NAV fell about 1%, in part due to net losses as well as overdistribution. The company generated 3 cents of accretion from share issuance above the NAV. This is a good result for investors, and it shows the company is ready to move quickly to boost the NAV with both share repurchases and additional issuance depending on valuation.

The NAV has been on a slight downtrend recently though it's not far off where it was in 2021.

CGBD completed the merger with CSL III which increased the portfolio to $2.5bn. This is a trend that we have seen across the sector with many BDCs, most notably OBDC and GBDC, completing mergers. The merger eliminated the preferred stock dilution overhang as Carlyle exchanged its investment at NAV. Carlyle also stumped up $5m for merger-related expensive coverage.

On the tariff side, management sees minimal potential direct risk to the portfolio.

Income Dynamics

Net new investments were pretty flat.

Leverage fell to the lowest level in a few years. The merger has created a lot of noise in the broader company's portfolio and leverage was one of these.

Both asset yield and interest expense fell during the quarter - common dynamics in the sector.

The assets acquired during the merger were the largest source of new assets during the quarter and were largely responsible for the drop in overall yield. With the merger out of the way and the Fed on the sidelines, the portfolio asset yield will be broadly stable from here.

Unusually in the sector, all of the company's liabilities are floating rate (we only recall NCDL and TSLX also having fully floating-rate liabilities in the sector).

The 2030 bond was recently swapped to a floating rate to begin in August. Because the 3-month / 5-year yield curve remains inverted (though not as much as it was, say, a year ago) it means that floating-rate debt is more expensive today relative to fixed-rate debt both in absolute terms as well as historical terms. CGBD will benefit more than the average BDC from the Fed continuing to cut the policy rate. However the expected inflationary impact of tariffs and continued strength in the labor market means that the Fed is in no hurry to cut.

Portfolio Quality

Non-accruals rose to a bit above the median level in our coverage (they remain below the average).

Portfolio quality, as measured by internal ratings, improved slightly.

There was a net realized loss during the quarter.

The allocation to first-lien loans has been growing at the expense of second-lien loans, which is good to see.

Valuation And Return Profile

Over the last year or so, the company has generated a return in line with the average in our coverage or slightly better.

This has translated into decent returns over various time horizons.

The valuation has been on a rollercoaster ride over the last couple of years, rising to as high as 116% before falling sharply to 84% (i.e. a 16% discount).

The stock went from trading as high as 5% above the average valuation in our coverage to a 15% discount to the average valuation which is slightly below the historical average.

The combination of decent performance and below-average valuation means the valuation-adjusted total NAV return is on the attractive side.

We initially allocated to the stock in 2021 when it was trading at a huge discount to the sector average. As it traded up in subsequent years, we reduced our allocation to zero. Recently, the stock's valuation fell sharply which we took advantage of to initiate a position once again.

CGBD remains more or less an average performer in the sector. This means that when its valuation traded up at the end of last year, there was little reason for investors to hold the stock. Now that the valuation is well below the average, it is once again an attractive option in the sector.

data.title: PayPal: Healthy FCF And Buybacks Can Improve Bullish Sentiment
data.description:
PayPal (PYPL) stock is trading almost 14% below the price it was at the start of 2025. Most of the tech stocks like Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) have recovered from tariff uncertainty. The broader S&P 500 is also in the positive for YTD. One of the reasons behind the bearish sentiment towards PayPal is the prediction of new competitive forces taking away market share from the company. Many big tech companies are looking to gain customers in the digital payments ecosystem. PayPal’s number of payment transactions has also dipped by 7% in Q1 2025 compared to the year-ago quarter, which has increased the cautious tone towards the company. However, the company continues to report healthy free cash flow and has projected FCF of $6 billion-$7 billion for the current fiscal with only $1 billion in capex. It is also investing massively in buybacks which should be a strong tailwind for EPS growth in the next few quarters. In the previous article, it was mentioned that PayPal's margin expansion could boost EPS in the first quarter, which is what the company reported in recent earnings.

The most positive factor working in PayPal’s favor is the valuation. The stock is trading at only 13.3 times the EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 and a mere 11.6 times the EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2027. There are no major capex requirements and a low PE multiple could allow the company to reduce outstanding shares rapidly, which can increase the attractiveness of the stock for long-term investors.

Mixed Bag Earnings Result

PayPal was able to beat EPS estimates by 15% in the recent quarter by boosting margins while missing on the revenue estimate by $50 million. The upcoming quarter has also seen a lot of EPS upgrades. There have been a total of 29 EPS up revisions in the past 90 days compared to only 1 down revision for the upcoming quarter.

Figure: EPS revisions for PayPal. Source: Seeking Alpha

PayPal has been able to deliver EPS beat for the last four quarters and beating the EPS estimates after recent up revisions in Q2 2025 could be a major boost for the sentiment. However, it should be noted that the company is certainly seeing intense competition in the digital payments ecosystem. This can end up hurting the sentiment even if the company is able to beat the EPS estimates by a wide margin.

Figure: Past EPS beat by the company. Source: Seeking Alpha

PayPal has been able to reduce transaction expense in the recent quarter which helped improve the operating income. We can see that the YoY revenue growth was marginal but there has been a steep decline in operating expense due to lower transaction expense. If the company can maintain the current trend, it could further boost the EPS trajectory.

Figure: Reduction in expense in recent quarter. Source: Company Filings

Gauging Potential Headwinds

In Q1 2025, PayPal reported a mere 2% YoY growth in active accounts and a 7% YoY dip in number of payment transactions. This decline in payment transactions is quite significant. The management has already announced that they would focus more on margins instead of increasing volume. A declining payment transaction volume shows that other competitors are increasing their market share. The digital payment ecosystem continues to evolve with new services being launched by both big tech companies and smaller startups.

Figure: Recent key metrics of PayPal. Source: Company Filings

However, despite the competition, I believe PayPal should be able to improve these key metrics in a few quarters. The company is launching new services and partnering with big businesses to provide seamless transactions to customers. The digital payments industry is likely not going to be a winner-takes-all service similar to the search engine business or Meta’s market share in the social media business. It is possible for different players to carve a niche for their service. PayPal has a good name recognition and high market share in key services. This should allow the company to compete effectively in the next few years, despite the warnings by some bearish analysts.

Healthy FCF And Buybacks Can Change EPS Trajectory

PayPal has given guidance for a free cash flow range of $6 billion to $7 billion in the current fiscal year. The capex estimate is a mere $1 billion, which shows that PayPal does not need to spend a massive amount similar to some other bigger tech players. The company has maintained the EPS target showing 6%-10% growth due to trade uncertainty. But it is highly likely that the annual EPS could beat the guidance, especially after the strong EPS beat in the first quarter.

Figure: FCF, share repurchase projections of PayPal. Source: Company Filings

The management has also announced a massive buyback program.

In the above chart, we can see that PayPal has reduced outstanding stock at a faster pace than Apple in the last three years. Apple is spending almost $100 billion annually on buybacks, but the impact is lower due to high PE multiple.

It is projecting share buybacks of almost $6 billion in the current fiscal year, which can reduce the outstanding stock by almost 8% at the current price. This would be a big tailwind for EPS growth. The lower PE multiple allows PayPal to show a bigger decline in shares.

Forward PE Valuation And Modest Multiple

Despite a low revenue growth projection, PayPal is expected to show almost 9.5% YoY EPS growth in the current fiscal and another 10.5% in the next fiscal year. The stock is trading at only 13.3 times the EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 and 11.6 times the EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2027. PayPal has the resources to deliver new services which can improve engagement with customers over the next few quarters. At the same time, healthy buybacks and a modest P/E multiple make the stock very attractive for long-term investors.

Figure: Forward EPS growth projections. Source: Seeking Alpha

PayPal's forward PE multiple is almost half of Apple's while the stock is showing a stronger EPS growth. As mentioned above, the buybacks will also boost PayPal's EPS growth in the near term compared to Apple's buyback program.

Investor Takeaway

PayPal has been able to beat EPS estimates in the recent earnings by 15%. The stock is still down YTD by 14% as Wall Street turns bearish over the ability of PayPal to fend off competition. PayPal did report a 7% YoY decline in the number of payment transactions. However, the company has been focusing more on margins instead of volume.

We could see the company show a massive reduction in outstanding stock in the next few quarters. This provides a floor to the stock price and also increases future EPS growth projections, making the stock a good option in this segment.

data.title: Legal & General: Expecting Double-Digit Returns In The Next 3 Years, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
One year after the strategic doubling down, the Legal & General Group Plc (OTCPK:LGGNF)(OTCPK:LGGNY) share price of this previously dormant giant has increased by nearly 30% (with a total return of >40%, including the dividend payment - Fig. 1). Here at the Lab, we also analyzed the company’s performance in late 2024 with an update called "Strongest Subsector Yield And Solvency Ratio," noting that the company's equity story gained renewed investor support on the back of several strategic initiatives. These included: 1) the planned divestiture of non-core assets, aimed at streamlining the business model, 2) a clear focus on capturing the growing institutional retirement market opportunity, 3) improved cost efficiency through cross-selling synergies following the consolidation of the asset management division, and 4) disciplined capital allocation, reflected in a progressive dividend policy and an ongoing share buyback program.

Fig 1

FY Results And Our Positive Take

Although L&G released its 2024 results in mid-March, we find reassurance in the tone and content of the latest press release. Despite the recent share price appreciation, we remain confident in the underlying equity story. We will briefly comment on the results before moving on to our forward-looking view.

In brief, L&G reported an EBIT profit of £1.61 billion, with a core EPS of 20.23p. Both outputs were up by 6% compared to last year. Institutional retirement policy reached £10.7 billion with a record volume in Canada and the USA. L&G is a leading asset management powerhouse with £1.1 trillion in assets under management [AUM]. The average fee rate increased by one basis point to 8 basis points. Additionally, the company's retail annuities policy continues to grow. On the balance sheet, L&G reached a 232% solvency II ratio, and we are not surprised to see a DPS up by 5% with a £500 million buyback announced for the year.

Source: L&G FY 2024 Results ppt - Fig 2

Why Are We Still Positive?

  1. L&G is "seeing positive commercial momentum" in annuities as well as in retirement policy. That said, what we believe L&G's potential gain will be in the Private Markets platform. This is relatively minor compared to the total company's AuM, but it is a growing division. The company recently launched new funds and strategic investments to drive margin expansion, broaden its product offering, and enhance overall return generation. Notably, when clients commit capital to private asset strategies, they are typically locked in for several years (10+2 years), with limited flexibility to reallocate without incurring break-up fees (usually at 5% after a lock-up period of almost 2 years). This long-term capital base not only enhances revenue visibility but also allows L&G to capture higher margins relative to traditional asset classes (liquid market investments). Although private assets currently account for less than 6% of total AuM (Fig. 3), we view this segment as a potential growth engine for the group over the medium to long term. To support our view, we found evidence that L&G signed the Mansion House Accord. This agreement encourages pension funds to increase allocations towards private assets;
  2. The company reported future profits of almost £15 billion based on gross profit on the Contractual Service Margin. Considering its elevated solvency position, the company is likely to support near-term shareholder capital returns despite the elevated payout ratio. For this reason, the company announced its intention to return more than £5 billion over the next three years. This represents approximately 35% of the current market cap (Fig. 4). This is the highest capital return in the subsector. L&G can fund this with a £17 billion per year growth in annuity volumes and its strong solvency position. Here at the Lab, we model a solvency position to fall to 195% by 2027, which is still above the regulatory requirements set at 180%;
  3. One of the L&G investor debates centered on balance sheet risks. Comparing leverage as a proportion of IFRS equity, the company ranks second in our universe in terms of investment leverage. That said, L&G's leverage is expected to decrease following the sale of the US protection business, and the company also exhibits the most significant negative sensitivity to a credit event.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Earnings Changes And Valuation

In our last update, we revised our target price to 260p, confirming a buy rating. This was due to CALA disposal, which reduced our EPS estimates by approximately 5%. Rolling forward estimates and assuming a 5% EBIT growth, we derived a core EBIT of £1.89 billion. This is also supported by UK interest rates, which have returned to Q1 levels after falling in April. In our view, this higher-for-longer environment is favorable for UK insurers. Considering unchanged technical guidance, the company anticipated a core operating EPS growth between 6% and 9% (Fig. 5). With no change in the tax rate (and a prolonged higher return on capital investment), we believe the primary driver of EPS growth will be a lower share count. This is well supported, as evidenced by the latest buyback. Our net profit reached £1.44 billion, and considering 5.78 billion of current shares outstanding, the ongoing repurchase (resulting in a 3.5% reduction for the year), our EPS increased to 26.4 cents from a previous forecast of 26 cents. Applying an unchanged sector median price/earnings of 10x (which is also based on L&G's historical average) (Fig. 6), our valuation reached £2.64 per share.

Fig 5

Fig 6

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) lower results from investment activities combined with credit risks, 2) lower results from the private markets margin, 3) lower interest rates, 4) volatile equity market, 5) higher regulatory requirements, 6) execution risks by combining the Legal and General Investment Management and Legal & General Capital divisions, 7) limited financial disclosure and 8) longevity risks.

Conclusion

We believe L&G is not capital-constrained, and we remain buy-rated, with a share price upside of more than 10% combined with a double-digit yield. We expect management to return more than 35% of the market capitalization to shareholders over the next three years, with the potential to accelerate EPS growth thanks to private market placements.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MLP Liquidity Vs. Other Infrastructure Alternatives
data.description:
Institutional investors have long favored infrastructure allocations for their steady cash flows, inflation sensitivity, and low correlation to traditional assets.

But they’ve historically accessed those benefits through private infrastructure deals. These are typical illiquid, high-minimum investments with long lockups and limited redemption windows.

For retail investors and advisors, that approach isn’t always practical, or even necessary. Publicly traded master limited partnerships (MLPs) offer many of the same attributes as private infrastructure, with fewer constraints.

In particular, we believe the liquidity of listed MLPs provides a tangible edge, enabling more flexibility when sizing positions, implementing asset allocation changes, or managing cash needs.

Here’s how MLP liquidity stacks up against two common alternatives: closed-end funds (CEFs) and interval funds.

Close-ended fund liquidity

Before ETFs dominated the investment landscape, CEFs were one of the primary ways to gain access to niche strategies like infrastructure. But structurally, they differ in important ways that impact their suitability for tactical or strategic allocations.

Unlike ETFs, CEFs don’t offer open-ended, in-kind creation and redemption of shares. Instead, they issue a fixed number of shares at IPO, and unless a secondary offering is conducted, that’s the entire float.

Investors can only buy or sell these shares on the secondary market, often with limited volume, and that itself can introduce liquidity challenges depending on demand.

One major quirk with CEFs is that their market price can diverge meaningfully from their net asset value (NAV). That means you might pay more than the underlying holdings are worth (a premium) or less (a discount).

This variability makes CEFs suboptimal, in our view, for expressing an infrastructure allocation. Instead of just making a call on the asset class, you’re also making a bet on market sentiment and trading patterns.

And it’s not enough to just “buy the discount.” Some CEFs trade at a discount for years, so what matters is how that discount or premium behaves relative to its own history.

This is typically assessed using a z-score, which measures how far the current premium/discount deviates from its historical average. A wide z-score may suggest a reversal is due, but it’s never guaranteed.

In short, what should be a straightforward allocation to infrastructure becomes a timing exercise: less about fundamentals and more about trading dynamics.

Interval fund liquidity

Another alternative some investors turn to for infrastructure exposure is the interval fund. These vehicles fall somewhere between traditional mutual funds and private placements.

While publicly registered, interval funds are not exchange-traded and do not offer daily liquidity. Instead, they allow redemptions only at preset intervals, usually quarterly, subject to a cap, typically around 5% of total assets.

The typical investor experience with interval funds goes like this: you subscribe during an offering period, lock your capital up, and then wait for the next redemption window.

If redemption requests exceed the allowed percentage, you might not get your money out right away, or at all, depending on demand. You’re essentially queuing with other investors and hoping there’s enough room to exit.

This lack of liquidity introduces serious challenges. It limits your ability to rebalance dynamically, scale exposure based on conviction, or respond to shifting market conditions.

And in periods of market stress, when liquidity matters most, interval funds can freeze entirely, leaving you locked in just when you may want or need to reallocate.

For advisors or individual investors aiming to manage portfolios tactically or with flexibility, interval funds can create more headaches than they solve.

MLP liquidity

In contrast to closed-end or interval funds, MLPs, especially the largest players by market capitalization offer excellent liquidity. Just look at a few examples from June 4:

  1. Enterprise Products Partners (EPD): bid-ask spread of $30.89 / $30.90, or just 0.03%

  2. Energy Transfer (ET): bid-ask spread of $17.54 / $17.55, also 0.03%

  3. Plains All American Pipeline (PAA): bid-ask spread of $16.85 / $16.87, or 0.12%

  4. Western Midstream Partners (WES): bid-ask spread of $37.59 / $37.63, or 0.11%

Many of these names trade millions of shares daily, are included in popular indexes, and are held in a range of ETFs and institutional portfolios. That results in a liquid secondary market with steady demand, helping ensure efficient execution even for large trades.

For investors and advisors looking to allocate to infrastructure with real-time pricing, transparent structure, and the flexibility to size or exit positions easily, we believe MLPs offer a highly accessible alternative to less liquid private strategies.

data.title: Cadence Design Systems: Where Next-Gen Chips Begin Their Journey
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) is at the center of semiconductor innovation, providing mission-critical chip design tools to industries. Although trading at a premium, its lasting competitive strengths and steady execution warrant a Buy rating for long-term growth investors willing to take on valuation risk.

Introduction

Cadence Design Systems will have a hand to play in almost every next-gen industry, empowering companies such as Apple, NVIDIA, Tesla, Amazon and Google with the tools to help build the future. The company is a leading provider of electronic design automation [EDA] software, hardware, and semiconductor intellectual property [IP]. Founded in 1988 based out of San Jose, Cadence has now become one of the “Big-Two” EDA companies worldwide alongside Synopsys (SNPS), enabling other companies to design and verify integrated circuits and electronic systems. Like its competitors, the company’s business model works on a subscription basis, with 80-85% of its revenue coming in from here itself. This model for the company is highly profitable, especially when all this revenue comes from long-term contracts and maintaining a renewal rate of 95% (Approx). The company’s tools are also mission-critical for chip design across various industries such as computing, communication, automotive, aerospace, as well as other end markets, which we will discuss further.

Unique Selling Point

Comprehensive product portfolio: The company offers a wide range of design tools as well as IP that cover nearly the entire chip development lifecycle - from electronic design, automation software for IC Design to Hardware verification platforms and predesigned IP cores. These tools are widely adopted industry standards, a few of them being Virtuoso (Analogue IC Design), Spectre (Simulation) and Allegro (PCB Design). This one-stop portfolio offered by Cadence makes them a long-term partner for semiconductor development. They do have a smaller portfolio than Synopsys, but a prime reason they are still chosen above them is, the company continuously infuses AI into its products, making them a first choice for any company, whether a Fabless designer (Like NVIDIA) or even an IDM (like Samsung) looking to make a breakthrough.

Strong Market Position and High Switching Costs: Cadence, along with Synopsys and Siemens, dominate the global EDA market, which is effectively an Oligopoly. These three companies control 80% of the EDA software in key market regions such as China. The company also makes sure to thoroughly innovate its ready-made IP portfolio to help companies save money and time. They are a strong choice for any new-gen company thanks to their strong AI-led innovation. Once designs are made using these IPs it makes it impractical for any company to switch providers for advanced chip projects, as it would not only need digital reworking but multiple aspects in the physical assembly as well.

AI-Driven Innovation - A Quiet Strategic Advantage: Cadence differentiates itself from Synopsys through faster AI development and a more targeted DSP portfolio. They are not only enabling AI chip design but also building a strong presence in the AI hardware landscape. The company has already made a name for itself with AI-specific IP, namely their Tensilica DSPs used in the Meta Ray-Ban glasses. Cadence combines AI-powered tools and AI-optimised IP in a way few others can, which will continue to give them an Edge down the line as they carry out their ongoing R&D. Cadence’s AI R&D efforts, though less publicised compared to its peers, are expanding their IP capabilities.

Direct Investor Earnings

Companies such as Cadence and Synopsys do not give out any dividends. This is simply because in a business like theirs, they prefer to focus on growth rather than paying out dividends. The companies give capital back to shareholders through share buybacks. This not only returns capital to the shareholders but also signals strong internal confidence, especially when Cadence repurchased roughly $150 million worth of stock in Q4 alone. In 2025, the company expects to deploy around 50% of the company’s free cash flow towards share buybacks. This means that with a projected cash flow of around $1.6 billion, with approximately $800M earmarked for share repurchases, it signals continued shareholder growth.

The implication of these share buybacks may be that the stock is not meant for income-oriented investors, as they do not receive any dividend, but it acts as a growth stock. Cadence uses its cash for R&D, strategic acquisitions (such as OpenEye), and stock buybacks. These actions of the company focus on R&D and shareholder value over short-term growth.

A crucial fact concerning the capital appreciation of the company is that the company maintains a client retention rate of 95%. This leads to consistent inflow and growth in revenue.

Risk Analysis

Business Related Risks:

Tight Competition And Market Share Erosion: Cadence operates in a very competitive market with its chief rival being Synopsys. If companies like Synopsys or Siemens EDA were to develop superior tools and target Cadence’s customer base, the company may face a huge problem. This is very unlikely to happen due to the duopoly nature of the industry. The bigger problem would be if the company were to remain “Stale”, where the company can no longer innovate or gain a first mover advantage and attract new customers, the longer the situation persists, the easier it would be for their customers to switch to Synopsys. Emerging competitors or open-source EDA designers could also remain a potential risk for the company as they go along to improve the quality of their software.

Customer Concentration and Industry Cyclicity: While the company does have a huge roster of customers, the semiconductor industry is itself very cyclical and somewhat concentrated. A small number of large-cap companies are responsible for a significant chunk of revenue for a company like Cadence. If one or more companies reduce their EDA spending or switch to competitors, it would take a toll on the company’s earnings. These big companies, such as Google and Meta, have started to develop their own in-house EDA capabilities for certain tasks, while they remain heavily reliant on companies such as Cadence and Synopsys, it may become a risk in the long run. While EDA spend is less cyclical than semiconductor hardware, there’s still risk to macro conditions. The company’s diversified client base offers some buffer against this industry cyclicity, as well as if a customer from one industry was to tighten up their R&D expenditure, their other customers could act resilient or further expand.

Overreliance on Hyperscalers for Long-Term Growth: Cadence’s long-term growth narrative relies heavily on hyperscalers like Google, Amazon, and Meta, who increasingly have started to design custom silicon. However, these firms are rapidly in-housing parts of the EDA stack, hiring experts and building internal RTL-to-GDS workflows. At the end of 2024, over 55% of AI inference compute on in-house chips, up by 20% from 2020. While Cadence still does look at retaining these clients, its share of workflow value may shrink as hyperscalers reduce their reliance on third-party tools. Investors may overestimate Cadence’s revenue sustainability in AI if they ignore this disintermediation in high-value verticals like IP reuse and Physical implementation.

Financial Risks

High Valuation and Market Expectations: Cadence’s stock trades at a premium, reflecting investors' expectations in accordance with its growth. At around $280 (as of writing this article), CDNS is valued at a 47x forward non-GAAP earnings along with 15x sales, which remains considerably higher than the current market. Even a minor deceleration in bookings or EPS could lead to a disproportionate re-rating.

Leverage and Capital Allocation: Cadence has historically had minimal debt, but has issued $2.5 billion of senior notes with an interest of 4.4% to inject cash into the company. While the company still has a net cash position of approximately $175 million (the net cash as of 2024 was $144 million), now with a debt obligation, Cadence will have to take a step back from pursuing large acquisitions to avoid any unexpected downturns in the form of high leverage. The company’s capital allocation could also be a potential concern if it overpays for acquisitions with low returns, leading to lower shareholder value. Along with these acquisitions come integration risk and goodwill write-downs if those deals underperform expectations.

Stock-Based Compensation Dilution: A long-term financial risk lies in the rise of stock-based compensation, which many don’t understand. Cadence recognised about $391 M (~9% of revenue) in stock-based compensation in 2024 that was not included in non‑GAAP earnings. This added to a GAAP–non‑GAAP EPS difference of ~$2.12 per share. Share repurchases with free cash flow, an estimated ~$1.26 B for the year, with ~50% dedicated to buybacks, accounted for management's expectation to mitigate this dilution. Yet if FCF deteriorates or valuations become higher, buybacks can come up short in eliminating dilution, threatening to reduce earnings quality and per-share value over the long term.

Earnings Analysis

With fiscal revenue growing 13.7% YoY to $4.641B and growth accelerating into the 1st quarter of the year as well, revenue hit $1.242B (+23% YoY), which exceeded estimates for the 4th time in the last 5 quarters. FY 2025 revenue is projected at 5.23B (+12%). Growth remains broad-based across products and regions, despite China’s notable decline in contribution due to export controls.

The business still remains software/IP driven, with 91% of its revenue coming from “Product and Maintenance”, with time-framed licenses contributing to around 85% of recurring revenue. Services revenue jumped by 67% to $428M, due to large consulting projects. The Regional revenue split is 50% from America, 20-25% from Asia excluding China, 15% from Europe and 10-12% from China

Cadence’s major strength remains to be its cash flow, In 2024, the company’s operating cash flow stood at $1.261B, converting to 1.12B in free cash flow. The balance sheet showed $2.64B in cash and $2.5B in debt, largely due to its September 2024 notes issue. Cadence remains to stand strong on cash and continues to stay net cash positive, as well as continuously buying back shares. Backlogs reached a record $6.8B in 2024, with $3.4B in current RPO ensuring a strong revenue visibility. With over 80% recurring revenue, expanding margins, robust EPs growth, and solid bookings already made well into the future, Cadence is well-positioned for sustained performance despite its macroeconomic uncertainties.

Competitor Analysis

For this, I will be comparing Cadence to its main rival, Synopsys. Not only is this company their main competitor, but they are also the largest EDA company in terms of revenue, reporting a revenue of $6.13B, making it 1/3rd larger than that of Cadence. Both companies have a very similar mix, providing a very similar business mix with comprehensive chip design software tools and semiconductor IP. Synopsys has historically been known for their digital logic design and also offers a large breadth of IP portfolio compared to Cadence, which has been strong in analog/mixed-signal design and PCBs. With the passage of time, each company has extended itself into the other’s domains, causing a significant overlap. This shows how both companies remain fierce rivals across the entire market. Talking about financial performance, both companies are growing well, with Synopsys capturing a revenue growth of 15% in FY24 compared to Cadence’s 13.5%. Both conduct similar businesses, hence bringing home similar margins as well. (Synopsys ~ 38% vs Cadence ~ 42%). The only difference between the companies in terms of product is Synopsys’ Software Integrity Group, which brings them 10% of their business, something Cadence has not explored fully. Overall, Synopsys and Cadence together run this Duopoly in the EDA segment. These companies control about 70-80% of the market, along with Siemens. Both companies have excellent pricing power and a high barrier to entry for new businesses to overtake them anytime soon. From an investment standpoint, both companies act as enablers for the broader semiconductor industry, offering secular exposure with a lower volatility. They are also valued similarly and hence have similar valuation multiples (Synopsys trades at 40+x earnings). As of now, competition remains intense, thriving and the companies will battle it out for every design win. A pricing war is unlikely given the mutual interest of both companies in high margins, but a technological shift to new open-source platforms may pose a problem for both companies.

Valuation

I conducted a five-year discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis of the company using the free cash flow to equity method and a cost of equity that reflects the company’s capital structure and long-term risk profile. The goal behind this is to assess the intrinsic value of the stock under conservative assumptions and finally do a relative analysis.

The model begins with FCFE of $1.85B in year 1, growing to 2.94B by year 5. This growth is particularly supported by revenue projections of $5.46B to $8.43B while maintaining a margin of 30-35%. Net capital expenditures are set to remain modest with 3% of CapEx debt-financed to reflect the company’s actual capital structure.

The FCFE cash flows are discounted using a cost of equity of around 10.90% derived by the CAPM formula (Risk Free Rate ~4.3%, Equity Risk Premium ~5.5%, and a beta of 1.20). The terminal growth rate has been set to 3% to estimate the value beyond year 5. This results in the present value of future cashflows to be $29.67B, translating to $108.68 a share.

From a relative valuation standpoint, Cadence does trade at a premium, but so do its competitors, such as Synopsys and Ansys. With a strong customer lock-in, high recurring revenues, and exposure to structural growth trends, especially in AI and 5G design, Cadence holds a strong position in a consolidated market with high barriers to entry and tough competition.

Who Is This A Right Fit For?

Cadence Design Systems is best suited for investors with a long-term growth-oriented mindset with a strong belief in the semiconductor and technological industry. Its “Mission-Critical” feature makes this company provide its investors with exposure in AI, 5G, and chip innovation. Investors comfortable with high valuation multiples, focused on quality, recurring revenue, and durable competitive advantages, may view Cadence as a strategic buy-and-hold. Deep value or income-focused investors may want to wait before they go to buy the stock due to its limited yield and premium pricing.

Conclusion

CDNS according to me, occupies a very important position in the future of semiconductor innovation. A robust subscription model, strong client retention, and mission-critical design tools make this company the need of the hour right now. While this company does have an elevated valuation compared to the market, the same is justified by the company in terms of quality and abundance of margin. This said, investors must be aware of its risks such as Industry cyclicity, growing in-house designs, and geopolitical uncertainties. Overall, I support a BUY rating for this stock.
